Correlation of BEAT indicators by MSOA

Author

Samuel Jones

Published

14 April 2026

data = transpose(data_r)


viewof outcome = Inputs.select(
["EME admissions for CHD", "EME admissions for heart failure", "EME admissions for myocardial infaraction", "EME admissions for stroke"],
{label: "Outcome (Y)"}
)

viewof predictor = Inputs.select(
["Prevalence of atrial fibrilation", "Prevalence of T2 diabetes","Prevalence of heart failure", "Prevalence of obesity", "Prevalence of stroke/TIA","Prevalence of hypertension",
"Prevalence of current smokers", "Deprivation (avg. score)", "Need for family food support (avg. rank)",],
{label: "Predictor (X)"}
)







// 3. Math Engine
// This block reactively calculates stats whenever the dropdowns change


stats = {
const x = data.map(d => d[predictor]);
const y = data.map(d => d[outcome]);
const n = x.length;
const sumX = d3.sum(x);
const sumY = d3.sum(y);
const sumXY = d3.sum(x.map((d, i) => d * y[i]));
const sumX2 = d3.sum(x.map(d => d * d));
const sumY2 = d3.sum(y.map(d => d * d));

const r = (n * sumXY - sumX * sumY) / Math.sqrt((n * sumX2 - sumX * sumX) * (n * sumY2 - sumY * sumY));
const r2 = r * r;
const absR = Math.abs(r);

// Strength Logic
const strength = absR > 0.8 ? "a very strong" :
absR > 0.6 ? "a strong" :
absR > 0.4 ? "a moderate" :
absR > 0.2 ? "a weak" :
absR > 0.1 ? "an extremely weak" : "no";

const direction = r > 0 ? "positive" : "negative";
const association = r > 0 ? "an increase" : "a decrease";

// R-Squared Meaning Logic
const meaning = r2 > 0.8 ? "indicating that the data fits the linear trend line very well, suggesting a highly predictive relationship. This means most areas follow the linear trend line closely." :
r2 > 0.6 ? "indicating that the data fits the linear trend line well, suggesting a strong predictive relationship. This means many areas follow the linear trend line closely." :
r2 > 0.4 ? "indicating a moderate fit to the linear trend line, suggesting a moderate predictive relationship. This means some areas deviate from the linear trend line." :
r2 > 0.2 ? "indicating a weak fit to the linear trend line, suggesting a weak predictive relationship. This means several areas do not follow the linear trend line closely." :
"indicating a very weak fit to the linear trend line, suggesting a very weak predictive relationship. This means many areas do not follow the linear trend line.";

return {
corr: r.toFixed(3),
r2: r2.toFixed(3),
strength,
direction,
association,
meaning
};
}
Plot.plot({
// 1. Add margins to make room for centered labels
marginLeft: 60,
marginBottom: 40,

// 2. Configure traditional axis labels
x: {
label: predictor,
labelAnchor: "center",
labelOffset: 35 // Moves label further down from the axis
},
y: {
label: outcome,
labelAnchor: "center",
labelOffset: 45 // Moves label further left from the axis
},

marks: [
Plot.dot(data, {
x: predictor,
y: outcome,
stroke: "steelblue",
fill: "steelblue",
channels: {
MSOA: "msoa21hclnm",
[predictor]: predictor,
[outcome]: outcome
},
tip: {
format: {
MSOA: true,
[predictor]: true,
[outcome]: true,
x: true,
y: true
}
}
}),
Plot.linearRegressionY(data, {
x: predictor,
y: outcome,
stroke: "red"
})
],
grid: true
})
md`<br>**Correlation coefficient:** ${stats.corr}<br>This suggests there is ${stats.strength} ${stats.direction} correlation whereby **${outcome}** is associated with ${stats.association} in **${predictor}**.<br><br>**R squared:** ${stats.r2}<br>The R squared value is a measure of how well the data fits the linear trend line. The R squared value in this case is ${stats.meaning}
<br><br><sup><small style="color: #6c757d;">Notes:<br>Indirectly standardised emergency admission ratio per 100,000 population for the 2020/21 to 2024/25 period.Source: SUS. Prevalence of GP-recorded long-term conditions indirectly standardised ratio as of February 2026. Source: Graphnet OHC. Deprivation is based on the average score of the Index of Multiple Deprivaiton 2025. Source: Ministry of Housing, Communities & Local Government. Need for Family Food Support is a sub-measure of the Priority Places for Food Index, 2024. Source: Consumer Data Research Centre at the University of Leeds, in collaboration with Which?`
